coredata.dc:title: Chitosan-derived N-doped carbon dots for fluorescent determination of nitrite and bacteria imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lili
Zhang, Hongmei
Wang, Yesheng
Xiong, Zhong
Zhao, Xihui
Xia, Yanzhi
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully synthesized via simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization using chitosan as carbon and nitrogen sources. The obtained N-CDs contained a variety of functional groups on the NCDs surface, and exhibited excitation-independent behavior and strong blue fluorescence with a relatively higher fluorescence quantum yield (QY = 35%). It also presented excellent water solubility, resistance to pH change, high ion strength and UV irradiation. Since the fluorescence of the N-CDs could be selectively quenched by NO2 –, they could act as a fluorescent sensor for the determination of NO2 – in real tap water and lake water samples with a wide linear range (1–500 μM) and low detection limit (0.1 μM). They could also be used for bacterial imaging as multicolor fluorescent probes. The results indicated that N-CDs could be a promising candidate material for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Chitosan
Fluorescence
Nitrite
Bacteria imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), the inset: HRTEM image, size distribution (B), XRD pattern (C), and FT-IR spectrum (D) of the N-CDs.
(A) AFM image of N-CDs. (B) Line scan height profile along the white line marked in (A). (C) Corresponding 3D AFM image.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs. (B-D) High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O1s, respectively.

(A) UV–Vis absorption, maximum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. (B) The photographs of N-CDs aqueous solution under visible light and UV light of 365 nm. (C) The (Ahν)2 vs hν curve. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths (300–390 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs towards various anion ions (300 μM). (B) Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 of N-CDs to various anion ions. F0 and F refer to the FL intensities of N-CDs at 400 nm in the absence and presence of anions, respectively. (C) Interference test of various anions and cations on relative FL intensities of N-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs adding various concentrations of NO2 – (0–500 µM). (B) The plot of F0/F versus NO2 – ions concentrations over the range of 1–500 µM. F and F0 represent the FL intensities of N-CDs at 400 nm with and without NO2 –, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves (λex = 330 nm, λem = 400 nm) of N-CDs without and with NO2 – (B) Cyclic voltammogram of N-CDs. (C) Energy level simulation of N-CDs. (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs + NO2 – and NO2 –.
Bright field and fluorescence images of E-coli incubated with N-CDs for 2 h. (A) under bright field, (B-D) 405 nm, 470 nm, and 560 nm excitation wavelength, respectively.
Schematic diagram of the hydrothermal synthesis and application of N-CDs.
Comparison of N-CDs as a fluorescence sensor for NO2 – detection with other reported methods.

head.ce:title: Chitosan-derived N-doped carbon dots for fluorescent determination of nitrite and bacteria imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles
Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L. Sun and H. Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully synthesized via simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization using chitosan as carbon and nitrogen sources. The obtained N-CDs contained a variety of functional groups on the NCDs surface, and exhibited excitation-independent behavior and strong blue fluorescence with a relatively higher fluorescence quantum yield (QY = 35%). It also presented excellent water solubility, resistance to pH change, high ion strength and UV irradiation. Since the fluorescence of the N-CDs could be selectively quenched by NO2 –, they could act as a fluorescent sensor for the determination of NO2 – in real tap water and lake water samples with a wide linear range (1–500 μM) and low detection limit (0.1 μM). They could also be used for bacterial imaging as multicolor fluorescent probes. The results indicated that N-CDs could be a promising candidate material for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Although nitrite (NO2 –) has been widely used in various fields, some of its negative effects on health including formation of carcinogenic N-nitrosoamine under acidic conditions in the stomach and methemoglobin in the blood [1–4]. The maximum limit of NO2 – in drinking water to be set as 65 μM by the World Health Organization (WHO) [5]. A simple, rapid method with high selectivity and sensitivity to monitor NO2 – in water is necessary [6]. Various methods have been developed including spectrophotometry [7], electrochemical method [8], colorimetry [9], capillary electrophoresis [10], Raman spectrometry [11], and chromatography [12]. However, the shortcomings of these methods including expensive instruments, complex operation or long detection time made them difficult to be widely used.

Fluorescence method based on quantum dots presents a great potential due to simple operation, fast response time, low cost, high sensitivity and selectivity [13–15]. Carbon dots (CDs) have become a promising candidate as fluorescence sensor because of their numerous attractive properties, such as simple preparation, remarkable photoluminescence, excellent photostability, good biological safety and high water solubility [16,17]. Although there are some reports on the use of CDs fluorophores to detect NO2 – [18–20], development of CDs fluorescent probes with low cost, high sensitivity and good selectivity to detect NO2 – is still challenging.

A variety of natural biomass, such as orange juice [21], walnut shells [22], Glinkgo leaf [23] have been used as a carbon source to prepare CDs. Even though they are cheap, green and sustainable, relatively low quantum yield and self-quenching problem of the CDs limit their applications. Heteroatom doping is an effective way to improve the fluorescence efficiency and stability of CDs [24]. It provides active sites to enhance the affinity of CDs to analytes, therefore, broadens its potential applications in the analysis and sensing [25]. Chitosan, a natural polysaccharide with high nitrogen content, is a promising candidate for the synthesis of N-doped CDs. Its polymer structure also made it easy to form continuous graphite structure in CDs [26]. Lv et al. reported synthesis of the N-doped CDs using chitosan and tartaric acid as carbon source and its utilization as a biocompatible fluorescent sensor for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA [27]. Gong et al. reported microwave-assisted chitosan-based N-doped CDs synthesis and their application for Fe3+ analysis and cell imaging [28]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is little information on the chitosan-derived N-doped CDs as fluorophore to selectively determine NO2 – in water.

The objective of this work is to use a simple one-step hydrothermal method to fabricate a high fluorescence intensity N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using chitosan as the precursor and characterize their properties. The as-synthesized N-CDs displayed high water solubility and excellent stability. A high N content (6.3%) was doped in the CDs, leading to the improvement of quantum yield (QY) to 35%. Furthermore, N-CDs was used as a fluorescent sensor to detect NO2 –. The selectivity and sensitivity to NO2 – were investigated by fluorescent quenching of the N-CDs. And the applicability of N-CDs for NO2 – determination in tap water and lake water was also explored. Additionally, N-CDs were utilized for multicolor bacteria imaging. Scheme 1 presents the hydrothermal synthesis and application of N-CDs.

Chitosan having 85% deacetylation degree was prepared in our lab using waste shrimp shells. The degree of deacetylation was determined by titration method [26]. All metal salts (BaCl2, AlCl3, FeCl2, FeCl3, MgCl2, CaCl2, HgCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, Ni(NO3)2, CuSO4, Co(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Na2SO4, NaNO2, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, Na2S2O3, Na2SO4, Na2S, NaH2PO4, Na3PO4, NaNO3, NaF, NaCl, KBr, KI) are analytical grade and were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China).

N-CDs was prepared using hydrothermal method. Chitosan (0.5 g) was dissolved in acetic acid solution (100 mL, 1%) to obtain chitosan solution, followed by filtering through a microporous membrane (0.45 µm) to remove insoluble substance. After filtration, chitosan solution (20 mL) was poured in an autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The as-prepared sample was filtered by microporous membrane (0.22 µm) and further centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove all deposits. The obtained yellow transparent solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed with a JEM-1200 EX (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscopy. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 10° to 80 using a D/MAX-RB X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra were determined by KBr method with scanning range 400–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1 using a NICO-LET5700 spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using a Nanoscope V Multimode 8 scanning probe microscope (Bruker Corp., USA) under ambient conditions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence (FL) were measured on a Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence decay curve was conducted with an Edinburgh FLS980 at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. Zeta potential value was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Zetasizer Nano ZSE (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).
Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was determined based on a method described by Zhang et al. [29] and the details were provided in Supplementary Material.

The prepared N-CDs solution (1.0 mg·mL−1) were diluted 10 times with ultra-pure water. The selectivity of N-CDs to NO2 – was evaluated by adding various ions, including anion ions (NO2 –, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, S2O3 2−, SO4 2−, S2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, NO3 –, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−) and metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Na+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+). The final concentration of each ion was 500 μM. The FL emission spectra were recorded at 330 nm excitation wavelength. The fluorescence response of the N-CDs to NO2 – and different interfering ions was analyzed. Interference experiments were performed under the coexistent conditions with concentration ratio of NO2 – to interference ion (500 μM) at 1:1.

The sensitivity of NO2 – detection was performed by adding different concentrations of NO2 – into the diluted N-CDs solutions. The FL emission spectra were recorded and the FL intensity of the maximum emission peak at 400 nm was used to quantitatively analyze NO2 – ions concentration.

Regarding the use of N-CDs to monitor NO2 – in water, water samples (both tap water in the lab and lake water from a still lake) were first filtered through 0.22 µm membrane, and then NO2 – with three known concentrations at 5, 10, and 25 µM respectively were added. The recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated.

Fluorescence imaging of bacteria was performed with N-CDs as a probe. E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) and B. subtilis (Gram-positive bacteria) were used as the model bacteria, and were inoculated in 50 mL sterile LB culture andshakenovernightat 37℃. A liquot (2 mL) was transferred into sterile centrifugetube and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min before the bacteria was collected and washed twice in PBS (pH = 7), followed by adding 0.5 mL N-CDs and incubating for 2 h to prepare the bacterial N-CDs conjugates. After that, the cells were washed twice with PBS to remove the excess N-CDs and dispersed in 0.5 mL PBS for fluorescence imaging. Fluorescence images were recorded on a fluorescence microscope (AXIO OBSERVER, CARL ZEISS, German) with excitation wavelength 405, 470 and 560 nm respectively. Cytotoxicity experiments were performed according to described by Hua et al. [30] and the details were provided in Supplementary information.

XPS was performed to investigate the elemental composition and chemical valence states of N-CDs. Fig. 3 A shows the XPS survey spectrum with three characteristic peaks at about 285, 400, 532 eV, which are attributed to the binding energy of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, and indicated the presence of C, N, O elements. The atomic ratios of C, N, O are 72.3%, 6.3% and 21.4%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) presents four fitted peaks at binding energy 284.3, 285.1, 285.9, and 287.0 eV, which are assigned to C-C/C = C (sp2 hybridized carbon), C-N, C-O (sp3 hybridized carbon), and C = O, respectively. This indicates that the graphitic structure of the prepared N-CDs with sp2 hybridized carbons atoms, and the presence of carbonyl groups. This observation might be associated with the fluorescence emission caused by the radiative transition of CDs [33]. For the N 1s spectrum, three fitting peaks located at 398.8, 399.7, 400.6 eV represent three types of nitrogen-containing group, namely, C-N-C (pyridinic-N), C = N (pyrrolic-N), N-H (graphitic-N) (Fig. 3C). The results further confirm the formation of the amide on the surface and the N-doping of the prepared N-CDs, which are responsible for the improving FL properties [34]. For the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D), the peaks at 530.4, 531.7, and 532.8 eV could be assigned to the C-O, C = O and -OH, respectively [35].

UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 A, black curve) displays a weak peak around 230 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* electron transition of C = C bond. The absorption peak at 290 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition of C = N/C = O bond [36]. It should be noted that the strong absorption band at 290 nm indicates that the content of C = N/C = O is very high, which is agreement with FT-IR and XPS results. The fluorescence spectra showed that the maximum emission wavelength was 400 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 4A). The N-CDs solution is yellow in visible light and bright blue fluorescence in 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4B). It is worth noting that the quantum yield of the N-CDs is 35%, which is relatively higher than that of most reported biomass-derived CDs [37,38]. The strong fluorescence property may be due to the doped heteroatoms and functional groups [39]. Further, the optical band gap (Eg) is related to the absorbance and photon energy [40]. Eg value of N-CDs was 4.47 eV, which could be estimated from the (Ahν)2 vs hν curve by extrapolating the linear fitting to the intercept of the energy axis, where A, h, and ν are the absorbance, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively (Fig. 4C) [41]. The excitation-dependent emission spectra of N-CDs shows that emission peak intensities enhanced gradually when excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 330 nm and then declined as excitation wavelengths further increasing from 340 to 390 nm (Fig. 4D). Themaximum emission peak also had anobvious red shift corresponding to the increase of excitation wavelength. The excitation-dependent emission behavior has been reported in previous studies, which might be due to the different size or surface functional groups of CDs [42–44].

Since fluorescence stability is very important for the application of N-CDs, the effects of high ion strength, UV irradiation and pH values on the FL intensity of the N-CDs were investigated. The N-CDS exhibited an excellent salt resistance and FL intensity was observed to remain stable with NaCl concentration up to 2 M (Fig. S1A). Furthermore, they also presented a satisfactory photostability and excellent stability in a wide pH range. FL intensity decreased slightly after continuous exposure to 365 nm UV lamp (8 W, 23 mW/cm2) for 2 h (Fig. S1B), and did not change significantly at pH 3 to 11 and only slightly decrease under under strong acidic or strong alkaline condition (Fig. S1C).

The selectivity of the N-CDs as fluorescence probe to NO2 – was investigated, while some potential interfering anions, including HCO3 –, CO3 2–, S2O3 2−, SO4 2−, S2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, NO3 –, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, were also tested under the same conditions. Fig. 5 A&B clearly show that only NO2 – caused a significant fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs, while the effects of other anions were negligible. Further, the interference of various anions and cations on the fluorescence detection of NO2 – was not significant (Fig. 5C). The results demonstrate the high selectivity of N-CDs to NO2 –.

The quantitative detection of NO2 – shows that the FL intensity of N-CDs gradually declined as NO2 – concentrations increased from 0 μM to 500 μM (Fig. 6 A). The plot of F0/F versus NO2 – ions concentrations over the range of 1–500 µM demonstrated an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.992) (Fig. 6B), indicating the N-CDs could be acted as sensitive fluorescence sensor for the quantitative detection of NO2 –. It should be noted that the linear behavior of quenching can be fitted by Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + KSV[C]), where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [C] is the corresponding concentration of NO2 –. The corresponding KSV for current work was calculated to be 1.4 × 103 L/mol. The limit of detection (LOD) for NO2 – was estimated to be 0.1 μM according to S/N = 3. When comparing the performance of monitoring NO2 – of our sensor with that of its counterparts in the previous reports−, our N-CDs presented a comparable or even better performance with a wide linear range (1–500 μM) and relatively lower detection limit (Table 1 ). This indicates the prepared N-CDs have a potential to be used as a fluorescent sensor for monitoring NO2 – with high selectivity and excellent sensitivity.

When the N-CDs was used as fluorescence sensor to determine NO2 – in tap water and lake water, the recoveries were found to range from 97.40% to 103.60%, and relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.80% (Table S1), indicating that the prepared N-CDs were relatively reliable and could be applied in detecting NO2 – in environmental water sample.

As shown in Fig. 7 A, the fluorescence decay lifetime of N-CDs remains almost unchanged even in the presence of 100 or 200 µM NO2 –, indicating static quenching mechanism without energy transfer between N-CDs and NO2 – [21].

The energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular (LUMO) of N-CDs were estimated with the empirical formula [51,52]:
E LOMO = -e (E Red + 4.4)
E HOMO = -e (E Ox + 4.4)
E HOMO = E LOMO - Eg

E red (reduction potential) of N-CDs was measured to be −0.30 eV by cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 7B), and the corresponding E LUMO was calculated to be −4.10 eV. Due to the irreversible oxidation behavior, Eox (oxidation potential) of N-CDs is difficult to obtain. E HOMO can be calculated to be −8.57 eV with the optical energy band gap (Eg), where Eg of N-CDs was 4.47 eV (Fig. 3 C). E HOMO and E LUMO of NO2 – were estimated to be −8.75 and −3.95 eV, respectively. No electron transfer occurred between N-CDs and NO2 – (Fig. 7C). The UV–Vis spectra of N-CDs remained almost the same before and after adding NO2 – (Fig. 7D), which indicated N-CDs did not react with NO2 – during the sensing process [18]. This result proved that ion binding effect is not dominant during the quenching process. The possible reason is that N-CDs is negatively charged with the zeta potential of −13.5 mV (Fig. S2). Strong electrostatic repulsion effect might prevent the adsorption of NO2 – on the surface of N-CDs [15,53]. A broad UV–Vis absorbance band was observed at about 360 nm (Fig. 7 D), which overlaps with FL excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs in the wavelength ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 4A). This could result in the FL quenching of N-CDs due to internal filter effect (IFE) [54]. Therefore, the overall fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs by NO2 – is a combination of static quenching and IFE.

N-CDs were investigated as multicolor fluorescent probe for bacterial imaging. Fluorescence imaging results revealed that E-coli and B. subtilis cells could be detected in three tested wavelengths and presented blue, green and red fluorescence (Figs. 8 and S3), which indicated that N-CDs can pass through the cell membranes or cell walls and stain the bacteria cells. It may be due to the unique functional groups on the surface and appropriate size of N-CDs. The results demonstrated that N-CDs could be utilized as a potential bio-imaging material. Additionally, the toxicity of N-CDs to bacteria was investigated via the microplate assay. The result of the toxicity of N-CDs to E. coli and B. subtilis shown in Fig. S4 displayed a 93% or higher the cell viability of bacteria at various concentrations of N-CDs (20–400 µg/mL), indicating the low cytotoxicity of N-CDs to bacteria. Therefore, compared with the traditional dyes and quantum dots, the prepared N-CDs are a better candidate for bio-imaging.

N-doped carbon dots were successfully synthesized using chitosan as both carbon and nitrogen sources via a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment. The obtained N-CDs displayed strong fluorescence and excellent water solubility and stability due to the tremendous oxygen and heteroatom functional groups. Furthermore, such N-CDs was applied as a fluorescent sensor to monitor NO2 –, exhibiting high selectivity and sensitivity with a wide linear range (1–500 μM) and low detection limit (0.1 μM). NO2 – result in the fluorescence static quenching of N-CDs through IFE. And this sensing system can be utilized to the determination of NO2 – in real water sample and has a potential to image bacterial cells.

Lili Sun: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Hongmei Zhang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yesheng Wang: Formal analysis. Zhong Xiong: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xihui Zhao: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yanzhi Xia: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51503110) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41, No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119468.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Heliyon.

ACS Sensors

Hou, Fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout sensor based on Griess assay for nitrite detection infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) with Spectrum GX Infrared and Microscopy, Spectrochimica Acta Part A : Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy. 225 (2020) 117470.

Anal. Chim. Acta
